items.prompt_md: In the greedy scan for counting non-overlapping intervals, why sort by **end** rather than by **start**?
options: Keeping the interval with the earliest end leaves the most room for future intervals, which is what the count depends on
Sorting by end avoids needing a comparison function at all
Sorting by end guarantees the intervals are already merged
Sorting by end makes the list shorter before scanning
items.answer: Keeping the interval with the earliest end leaves the most room for future intervals, which is what the count depends on
items.explanation_md: A long interval that starts early but ends late can block more future intervals than a short one that ends sooner. Sorting by end and always keeping the one that finishes earliest is the greedy choice that never blocks more than necessary.

items.prompt_md: You need the maximum number of intervals from a list that don't overlap each other (equivalently, the minimum number to remove so none overlap). Which approach fits?
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
Sort by start time and merge each interval into the last one whenever it overlaps
Try every subset of intervals and check each one for overlaps
Build a prefix-sum array over interval endpoints and scan for the maximum
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
items.explanation_md: Sorting by end and greedily keeping whatever is compatible with the last kept interval's end is the classic interval-scheduling-maximization greedy: the earliest-ending compatible choice never blocks more future intervals than any alternative.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the sort-by-end greedy scan over n intervals?
items.explanation_md: The scan that keeps or drops each interval is a single O(n) pass, but sorting the intervals by end dominates at O(n log n), which is the overall bound — far better than the O(2^n) brute-force subset search.
